title: Feldkurs Schweiz
text:
Learning objectives

The students are familiar with the different types of droughts that affect Switzerland (hydrological, meteorological, and agricultural).
The students know how droughts are monitored both in-situ and using remote sensing and meteorological observations and know how to analyse drought monitoring data.
The students know about the impacts of droughts on agriculture and forests in Switzerland.
The students are familiar with drought adaptation strategies for various sectors in Switzerland.

title: Feldkurs Schweiz
text:
Target audience and prerequisites
Master students in Geography at the University of Bern.

This course is taught by Prof. Olivia Romppainen-Martius, Prof. Adrien Mestrot, and Prof. Benjamin Stocker and assisted by Ananda Kurth.

title: 1  Course design
text:
1.1 Theme and context
Extreme weather requires adaptations in the management of natural resources, including water resources, biodiversity, soil cultivation in agriculture, and planning for climate-smart forestry. Information and near-term predictions of climate impacts are generated through diverse continuous measurements and remote sensing of the atmosphere, hydrological systems, soils, and ecosystems. In combination with extended-range numerical weather prediction, this information can inform planning and interventions on a variety of levels. The recently launched National Drought Platform (https://www.trockenheit.admin.ch) gathers such information and makes it publicly available in a form that satisfies diverse demands by different stakeholders.
This field course familiarizes students with a multitude of observational system and measurement types, provides hands-on experiences with measurements in the field, and lets students take the perspective and learn about practices and demands by various stakeholders.

title: 1  Course design
text:
1.2 Goal
The course consists of a preparatory phase, and a field phase. During the preparatory phase, information on the current (i.e., weeks leading up to the field phase) level of drought, considering a set of relevant Earth system variables, is gathered and interpreted in the context of the climatology (past 30 years), of medium (15 d) to seasonal forecasts, and of climate change projections.
The field phase consists of visits at several institutions, research sites, and localities that deal with droughts, their impacts, and adaptation. Students will familiarise themselves with the different perspectives from different stakeholders and learn about processes that drive drought impacts in different natural spheres and socio-economic domains.

title: 2  Course plan
text:
2.1.1 Afternoon session 1 (20.04.2026)

Course logistics, programme, learning objectives
Introduction to summer droughts in Switzerland, drought monitoring
Identification of essential variables
Starting points for data download, analysis, and interpretation. Analysis should put current state into a multi-year reference
Approach to collecting stakeholder perspectives, guiding field visits



2.1.2 Afternoon session 2 (11.05.2026)

Support for data download, analysis, and interpretation
Consolidate questions stakeholders to a common set
Preparation/guidance for presentation (structure)
